Q1 — Place: The little workbench in my father's garage where he kept his watch repair tools. Tiny screwdrivers in a felt-lined drawer, a brass loupe, a marble slab he'd use to lay out parts so they wouldn't roll away. I wasn't allowed to touch it, but I'd just stand and look. The way the light fell on the brass tools and made them look surgical. He'd let me watch sometimes when he was opening the back of a watch — the whole world reducing to a 4-inch circle and the way his hands didn't shake. I remember thinking that what he was doing was a kind of secret language only patient hands knew.
Q2 — Habit: I peel oranges in one continuous spiral. I can't help it. If I have to cut something, I align the knife perfectly and pre-visualize the cut before making it. I tie my shoes the same way every time — the loops symmetrical down to a millimeter. My partner thinks I'm OCD; I don't think it's about anxiety. It's that my hands seem to want to do things cleanly. If I drop a single grain of salt on the counter, I notice it before it hits, and I'll wipe the counter twice because I can still see where it landed. The world feels different to me when small things are precisely in place.
Q3 — Explanation: I'll explain how a sewing needle and thread actually pull fabric together. People think the needle pierces and the thread follows. What's actually happening: the needle creates a temporary geometric pathway through the fibers, the thread fills it, and the tension on the thread re-aligns the fibers around the new shape. You're not stitching the fabric — you're re-organizing its internal architecture. A bad stitch leaves the fibers misaligned, which is why cheaply-sewn things unravel: the geometry is wrong, not the thread.
Q4 — Conflict: I lost a friendship over a kitchen disagreement. I was helping her cook for a dinner party and she was chopping vegetables in this loose, casual way — uneven sizes, the knife rocking too far, fingers in danger. I quietly took over without asking. She said, with real anger, that I always make her feel inadequate. The surface story was about chopping. The real story was that I genuinely could not bear watching her chop — it felt physically painful to me, like watching someone misaligned. I didn't know how to explain that without sounding contemptuous. She thought I thought I was better. I just couldn't tolerate the imprecision.
Q5 — Sound: The very specific tick of a mechanical watch in a quiet room. Not the second hand visible movement — the actual escapement, the tiny click of metal-on-metal that happens about five times per second. Most people don't notice it. I find it incredibly satisfying. It's the sound of something working exactly to specification, over and over, without anyone watching. I once kept an old watch by my bed for a year just to fall asleep to it. The rhythm was a promise.
Q6 — Body: I was at a dinner party and watched someone start to drop their wine glass. Before I'd consciously seen it slip, my hand was already moving toward the table at the right angle to intercept. I caught it about three inches above the floor with the precise grip you'd use to hold a baby bird. My partner watched me do it and said 'how did you—' and I genuinely didn't have an answer. My hand knew the trajectory, the right amount of force, the right surface area, before I had any thoughts about saving the glass.
Q7 — Unworded: The feeling of being completely absorbed in something so small and exact that the rest of the world goes quiet. Not loud absorption — not the kind where you forget to eat. The opposite. Total stillness inside, total concentration in a 6-inch field. I get there when threading a needle, sometimes when peeling a tangerine without breaking the membrane, occasionally when realigning a screw. People who don't experience this often ask if I'm OK — they read the stillness as concerning. It's actually the most centered I ever am.
